recipes.name: Vektro 100
recipes.film: Original creation, no film ancestor — high contrast, cool tones, subtly balanced color

recipes.name: Fuji Pro 400H
recipes.film: Discontinued pro color negative (2004-2021) — cool tones, soft skin, a wedding favorite
recipes.tip: Prefer warmer tones? Simply raise the temperature by 300-500 Kelvin.

recipes.name: EvPro+
recipes.film: Original all-rounder profile, not based on a film stock — soft gradations, strong in low light
recipes.tip: Adjust Black level to taste: around -9 in daytime, -2 at night; raise it for a faded, dreamy look.

recipes.name: Kodak Ektar 100
recipes.film: Ultra-fine-grain color negative (2008) — the most saturated C-41 film, loves bright light
recipes.tip: Shines in bright light — expose correctly and the colors get richer and more vibrant.

recipes.name: Kodak Gold V1
recipes.film: Consumer color negative of the 80s-90s — warm, golden, the look of family albums
recipes.tip: Go hot with white balance — around 7800K with A5-G3 or A7-M1 — and expose for your subject.

recipes.name: Kodak Gold V2
recipes.film: Kodak Gold reimagined toward Vision3 200T — teal skies and golden warmth, Asteroid City vibes
recipes.tip: At home in bright daylight, especially golden hour; soft contrast keeps portraits flattering.

recipes.name: Kodak Portra 800
recipes.film: High-speed daylight color negative — fine grain, natural skin tones, thrives in low light
recipes.tip: Easy to underexpose by accident — set +1 to +2 EV for clean, well-balanced shadows.

recipes.name: Kodak Portra 400
recipes.film: The gold-standard portrait color negative — natural skin tones, soft balanced color
recipes.tip: Very forgiving — slight under- or overexposure won't hurt; a great pick for beginners.

recipes.name: Reggie's Portra
recipes.film: Reggie Ballesteros' take on Kodak Portra 400 — balance of film aesthetic and digital clarity
recipes.tip: Underexpose a third to half a stop to match Fuji exposure; add a diffusion filter for a dreamier, softer look.

recipes.name: Kodak Color Plus 200
recipes.film: Budget classic since 1972 (Kodacolor II) — fun, vibrant, gritty grain like Kodak Gold
recipes.tip: Pairs beautifully with a black mist diffusion filter for a soft, film-like glow.

recipes.name: Kodak Ultra Max 400
recipes.film: Consumer all-rounder negative (2007, replaced Gold 400) — the look of early-2000s snapshots
recipes.tip: The author typically underexposes by 0.3-0.7 stops in daytime; skin roll-off stays nice even overexposed.

recipes.name: Kodak Ektachrome
recipes.film: Kodak slide film — rich, deep colors that famously shift with light and exposure
recipes.tip: Expose to the right for warm, saturated color; underexposing shifts the whole image toward magenta.

recipes.name: Kodachrome 64 V1
recipes.film: Legendary slide film (1935-2009) — deep saturated color that documented an entire century
recipes.tip: For the vintage look, raise temperature 200-300K and nudge the color filter gently toward Blue-Magenta.

recipes.name: Kodachrome V2 (Gold)
recipes.film: Golden Age 1950s Kodachrome — golden skin, velvet blue cast, Saul Leiter's palette
recipes.tip: Uses a floating Kelvin value: run 3900K for cooler tones, up to 5000K to emphasize golden skin.

recipes.name: Kodachrome V3
recipes.film: Rarer Kodachrome slides with warm tones and teal skies

recipes.name: Kodachrome V4
recipes.film: 1976 Joel Meyerowitz-era Kodachrome — desaturated, magenta-tinted vintage look

recipes.name: Nostalgic Neg (Sony)
recipes.film: Original recipe inspired by faded family prints — pink, orange and olive, not a film replica

recipes.name: Fuji Nostalgic Neg
recipes.film: Fujifilm's simulation of 1950s Kodachrome — golden skin, warm reds, dusty blue skies

recipes.name: Leicachrome
recipes.film: Modeled on Leica M9 CCD colors — deep, vibrant, with rumored Kodachrome kinship
recipes.tip: Underexpose slightly for velvety skies; overexpose and the blues shift toward a striking cyan.

recipes.name: Classic Cinema
recipes.film: Original recipe channeling 90s movies shot on Kodak film — warm retro feel
recipes.tip: Set AWB with the given color filter and P mode at -0.5 EV; shift Color Phase +1/+2 if magenta skies bother you.

recipes.name: Classic Chrome
recipes.film: Fuji's homage to old photojournalism chrome films — muted tones, relaxed cyan-leaning blues
recipes.tip: Try early morning or late afternoon light to bring out its nostalgic, cinematic feel.

recipes.name: Velvia
recipes.film: Fuji slide film (1990) — vivid saturation, deep contrast, the landscape legend

recipes.name: Fuji Fortia 50
recipes.film: Limited-run Fuji slide film famed for extreme, deep vivid color
recipes.tip: Made for experimenting — over- or underexpose and shift temperature for wild color shifts.

recipes.name: Provia
recipes.film: Fuji color reversal film — fine grain, vivid yet balanced color reproduction
recipes.tip: Very forgiving — the author overexposes +0.5 to +1 stop; around 4500K is the temperature sweet spot.

recipes.name: Acros X
recipes.film: Fuji Acros B&W film recreated on Sony — with X/Y/R/G filter variants, nearly indistinguishable

recipes.name: Sony Classic Negative
recipes.film: Sony-only twist on Fuji's Classic Neg — warm nostalgia, faded shadows, a focus on reds
recipes.tip: Indoors it can run too warm — drop the temperature by 700-1500K to balance ambient light.

recipes.name: Reala Ace
recipes.film: Fuji simulation of Fujicolor Superia Reala 100 — natural, restrained, minimal-edit look
